Mid-Nineteenth Century Jewish Newspaper Subscribers

17,689 subscriptions · 4 newspapers · 1843–1868 · 952 places
Occident
Israelite
Messenger
Weekly Gleaner
Circle size = subscriber count · Toggle papers · Click for detail

Places where only one newspaper had any subscribers — 701 towns out of 952 total. The Israelite dominates (511 towns), reflecting its broad Midwestern reach into smaller communities where no other paper competed. Toggle papers to compare geographic footprints. Grey circles = shared towns (2+ papers) shown for context.

Circle size = subscriber count · Click for detail

Monopoly towns by newspaper

Number of towns where each paper was the sole subscriber source.

Monopoly subscriptions by newspaper

Total subscription rows in monopoly towns per paper.

Year-level subscriber counts. Toggle normalized to adjust for archival survival rate (raw count ÷ coverage %). Toggle coverage to see what fraction of expected issues have surviving records — years below 50% are marked and counts should be treated with caution.

Coverage = % of expected weekly/monthly issues represented in subscriber records

Archival issue coverage per paper per year

Percentage of expected issues (weekly = 52, monthly = 12) with at least one subscriber record. Bars below 50% indicate sparse archives; treat those year counts as lower bounds.

Subscribers per year by newspaper

Absolute counts. Diamond markers = years with under 50% archival coverage.

Proportional share by year

Relative balance over time.

Individual vs. Business by newspaper

Final_Classification field (97.8% complete).

New subscriptions per week using the Datetime field. Agent-submitted rows flagged separately when enabled.

States grouped into six regions. The Occident skews Mid-Atlantic and Southern; the Israelite dominates the Midwest; the Messenger is almost entirely Mid-Atlantic; the Gleaner is exclusively Western.

Total across all years.

Proportional breakdown of each paper geographic base.

Multi-paper states: subscriber split

Only states where two or more papers had subscribers.

The Subscriber Type field is partially populated. The Occident has the most complete gender data; the Messenger is almost entirely untyped except for Clergy rows.

Subscriber types per newspaper (typed rows only, as % of typed)

Male, Female, Business, Clergy, Synagogue/Org, Military/Political.

Clergy and institutional subscribers over time

Synagogue, Organization, Clergy, and Scholar rows per year.

Type-field completeness by newspaper

How many rows have a Subscriber Type vs. total rows.

A flow is recorded when a subscriber name appears in paper A before paper B (by first-appearance year), using edited names across the full dataset, not UUID only.

Total subscriber name flows between papers (all years)

Curve thickness proportional to number of names. Numbers shown on flows of 30 or more.

Hover flows or paper nodes for detail · Click a node to focus that paper

Occident subscribers first appearing in other papers, by year

The 1857 spike shows the Messenger launch pulling Occident readers.

Weekly Gleaner subscribers — where they went

Where Gleaner names next appear after the paper ends.

Newspaper agents collected subscription payments and recruited new readers on behalf of publishers. Diamond markers show agent locations by paper; background circles show subscriber concentrations for geographic context. Use the toggles to isolate individual papers.

Occident
Israelite
Messenger
Weekly Gleaner
Diamond = agent · Click for details · Background circles = subscriber counts

The Weekly Gleaner launched with a blitz of 36 agents in 1857 then rapidly disappeared; The Israelite built steadily to a peak of 59 agents in 1861; The Messenger grew sharply to 38 in 1859. Agent tenure data is available for agents with start and end years recorded.

Active agents per year by newspaper

Count of agents with recorded appointments that year.

Top cities with agents from multiple papers

Cities where two or more papers placed agents — showing competitive agent deployment.

Agent tenure distribution

How many years each agent served (agents with both start and end dates).

Subscriber coverage by agent city

Percentage of each paper's subscriptions that came from cities where that paper had a named agent.

Paper-competition network: shared agent cities

Each paper is a node; edges connect papers that deployed agents in the same city. Edge thickness and label = number of cities where both papers had agents. Click an edge to see the shared cities.

Agent recruitment network: top agents & their subscriber cities

Left nodes = top agents by total subscribers recruited (sized by total); right nodes = cities they served (sized by subscribers). Line thickness = subscribers recruited in that city. Color = paper. Hover for details.

How might agents have moved between cities to recruit subscribers? The left panel shows a force-directed network of top agents (colored diamonds) and subscriber cities (grey circles) they served — edge thickness proportional to recruits. Click an agent node to see their probable travel route on the right map, overlaid with 1850s–60s infrastructure. Historical routes are approximate reconstructions from period maps.

Transport layers:
Click an agent node to trace their route — sized by recruits — drag to rearrange
